content.text: In reinforcement learning, every decision an agent makes is eventually judged by a scalar signal called a
reward
. The reward at a single step is rarely what the agent actually optimizes — instead it optimizes the
return

, the discounted cumulative sum of all rewards from a given point onward. This distinction between instantaneous reward and long-term return underpins every RLHF training loop: the reward model produces a terminal score for a full response, and the algorithm must propagate that sparse signal backward in time so every generated token learns from it.
What Rewards and Returns Are
A
reward
is a scalar value
that the environment emits after the agent takes action
in state

. It measures the immediate desirability of that action — positive for good actions, negative for bad ones, and zero when the outcome is neutral or unknown. In an LLM generating a response, the environment is trivially simple: each token generated produces
until the response is complete, at which point the reward model assigns a single terminal score.
A
return
is the total discounted reward accumulated from time step
until the end of the episode. It aggregates all future rewards, with a discount factor
that controls how much future rewards are down-weighted relative to immediate ones:

attrs.latex: G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t} \gamma^k \, r_{t+k} = r_t + \gamma r_{t+1} + \gamma^2 r_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{T-t} r_T

content.text: The return is recursive:
. This one-step backup relationship is what makes the Bellman equation and TD-learning possible. In the RLHF setting with sparse terminal reward and
, the return simplifies:
for all
because all intermediate rewards are zero.
Why Rewards and Returns Matter
The return

is the actual quantity RL algorithms optimize. While the reward function is provided by the problem designer (or in RLHF, trained from human preferences), the return is what the policy gradient theorem differentiates through. Every policy gradient algorithm — REINFORCE, Actor-Critic, PPO — is ultimately computing some estimate of the return and using it to determine which actions to encourage or suppress.

Understanding the reward structure clarifies several critical design decisions in RLHF: why a KL penalty is necessary, why the discount factor is set to 1.0, why advantage normalization is standard practice, and why the critic network must learn to predict returns rather than rewards. Getting any of these wrong produces training instability, reward hacking, or degenerate policies that score well on the reward model while generating incoherent text.
In the language of the MDP, the policy optimization objective is the expected return from the initial state distribution:
Maximizing
requires computing or estimating
for sampled trajectories — which means understanding exactly how rewards accumulate into returns.
Benefits
Holistic evaluation of sequences.
Returns aggregate information across the full trajectory. A terminal reward score captures whether an entire response is helpful, harmless, and honest — something impossible to measure per-token.
Principled credit assignment.

The return combined with a value function and GAE distributes the terminal signal across all tokens in the response. Tokens that contribute to high-quality responses receive positive advantage; tokens that degrade quality receive negative advantage.
Compatibility with policy gradient methods.
The return provides the signal needed for the policy gradient theorem. With
, gradient ascent directly increases the probability of actions that led to high returns.
Controllable optimization pressure via discount factor.
The discount factor
lets the practitioner tune how far-sighted the agent is. For RLHF,
means every token in the response is equally responsible for the terminal reward.
Natural alignment with human feedback.

Humans evaluate complete responses, not individual tokens. Sparse terminal rewards match how evaluators naturally provide feedback, avoiding the need to artificially decompose quality into per-word scores.
Trade-offs
Credit assignment difficulty.

With sparse terminal rewards, a 200-token response receives one reward signal. Distributing that signal backward to individual tokens is imprecise. The value function and GAE partially solve this, but the quality of credit assignment is limited by how well the critic learns to predict returns at each position.
High gradient variance.

Monte Carlo returns (using the actual trajectory reward as the target) have no bias but very high variance — the same prompt can produce very different responses and therefore very different return values. This requires large batch sizes and careful normalization to get stable gradients.
Reward hacking.

The policy can find patterns that maximize the reward model's score without producing genuinely good text. Without a KL penalty in the return, the policy drifts arbitrarily from the reference model and degrades fast.
Sample inefficiency.

Each episode requires a complete forward pass (autoregressive inference) through the full response, followed by reward model inference. This makes RLHF orders of magnitude more expensive per gradient step than supervised fine-tuning.
Sensitivity to reward scale.

The magnitude of the reward model's outputs affects advantage scale, which affects gradient step sizes. Without normalizing rewards, training is sensitive to the reward model's output range and can diverge or under-update.
How It Works: The Mathematics
The Reward Function
The reward function
assigns a scalar to each (state, action) pair. In standard RLHF, this is a
sparse terminal reward
— non-zero only at the final token. The full RLHF reward at the terminal step combines the reward model score and a KL divergence penalty:

attrs.latex: R(x, y) = r_\phi(x, y) - \beta \cdot \mathrm{KL}\bigl(\pi_\theta(y \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_\mathrm{ref}(y \mid x)\bigr)

content.text: Where
is the reward model with parameters
, and
is the KL coefficient (typically 0.01 to 0.1). The KL term measures how far the current policy has drifted from the reference model and penalizes large deviations.
Per-Step KL Rewards

A key implementation detail: rather than computing the full-sequence KL divergence and adding it only at the terminal step, RLHF implementations distribute the KL penalty over individual token positions. This converts the purely sparse reward into a mildly dense reward:

attrs.latex: r_t = \begin{cases} -\beta \log \dfrac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\pi_\mathrm{ref}(a_t \mid s_t)} & t < T \\ r_\phi(x, y) - \beta \log \dfrac{\pi_\theta(a_T \mid s_T)}{\pi_\mathrm{ref}(a_T \mid s_T)} & t = T \end{cases}

content.text: The sum
equals the sequence-level log ratio

by the chain rule of probability, so distributing the KL this way is mathematically equivalent to the terminal formulation. The benefit is practical: non-terminal steps now carry a small non-zero reward, which gives the value function more signal to learn from and reduces the variance of advantage estimates at early steps.
Return Calculation with the Discount Factor
For RLHF with
and per-step KL rewards, the return from step
is:

attrs.latex: G_t = \sum_{k=t}^{T} r_k = \underbrace{-\beta \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \log \frac{\pi_\theta(a_k|s_k)}{\pi_\mathrm{ref}(a_k|s_k)}}_{\text{partial KL from step }t} + r_\phi(x,y) - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(a_T|s_T)}{\pi_\mathrm{ref}(a_T|s_T)}

content.text: Early in training, the KL term is small (the policy has not yet drifted far from the reference model), and the return is dominated by the reward model score. As training progresses and the policy diverges more from the reference, the KL terms grow and act as a brake on further divergence.
Monte Carlo vs. Bootstrapped Returns
There are two ways to estimate
from collected trajectory data.
Monte Carlo
uses the actual observed rewards summed to the episode end:
. This is unbiased but has high variance.
TD (bootstrapped)
truncates the sum and uses a value function estimate for the tail:
. This is biased by the critic's approximation error but has lower variance.
In practice, RLHF uses Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE), which interpolates between Monte Carlo and TD-1 via a parameter
. The GAE advantage is:
The return target used to train the critic is then
. This is the most common return estimation method in PPO-based RLHF implementations.
Architecture and Flow

The diagram below shows how rewards and returns flow through one RLHF episode. Each generated token receives a per-step KL penalty; the terminal token additionally receives the reward model score. The return from any step aggregates all subsequent rewards.

attrs.alt: Rewards and Returns in RLHF: top panel shows one episode trajectory with sparse terminal reward rT and per-step KL; middle panel compares discount factors gamma=0, 0.95, 1.0; bottom panel contrasts dense vs sparse reward bar charts.

content.text: When to Use Each Reward Strategy

Sparse terminal rewards (the RLHF default) are appropriate when the task outcome is holistic and human evaluators naturally rate complete outputs rather than individual steps. This is the case for response helpfulness, instruction following, and safety — qualities that emerge from the full response, not from individual tokens.

Dense rewards are better when per-step quality is well-defined and measurable. Examples include code execution with syntax checking after each line, mathematical reasoning with intermediate verification, or search agent tasks where each retrieved document can be scored for relevance. Dense rewards accelerate learning by providing stronger gradient signal at each step but require designing a step-level reward function that does not misalign with the actual objective.

Process reward models (PRMs), which score each reasoning step in a chain-of-thought, are an emerging middle ground. They provide denser signal than a terminal-only reward while remaining more tractable than token-level reward design.
When Not to Use Sparse Rewards
When episodes are very long (thousands of tokens) and credit assignment becomes prohibitively noisy — a dense or process reward model is preferred.
When intermediate correctness is critical and detectable, such as multi-step mathematical derivations where an error in step 3 propagates to step 10.
When exploration is required — a policy that has never received a positive signal cannot improve with sparse rewards unless it happens to stumble onto a rewarded trajectory.
How to Implement Rewards and Returns

A complete implementation requires three components: a reward function that combines the reward model score with per-step KL penalties, a return calculator that runs GAE over the full trajectory, and a return normalizer that stabilizes gradient magnitudes.

import torch
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Trajectory:
    """All data for one complete episode."""
    prompt_ids:       torch.Tensor   # (T_prompt,)
    response_ids:     torch.Tensor   # (T_resp,)
    policy_log_probs: torch.Tensor   # (T_resp,) — log π_θ(a_t|s_t)
    ref_log_probs:    torch.Tensor   # (T_resp,) — log π_ref(a_t|s_t)
    values:           torch.Tensor   # (T_resp,) — V(s_t) from critic
    rm_score:         float          # scalar: reward model score for full response


def compute_rewards(
    traj: Trajectory,
    kl_coef: float = 0.05,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Build the per-token reward vector.
    - Non-terminal steps: negative per-token KL penalty only.
    - Terminal step: RM score minus terminal KL penalty.
    Returns shape (T_resp,).
    """
    T = len(traj.response_ids)
    kl_per_token = traj.policy_log_probs - traj.ref_log_probs  # log(π_θ/π_ref)

    rewards = -kl_coef * kl_per_token          # shape (T,): KL at every step
    rewards[-1] += traj.rm_score               # add RM score at terminal step
    return rewards


def compute_returns_and_advantages(
    rewards: torch.Tensor,
    values:  torch.Tensor,
    gamma:   float = 1.0,
    gae_lam: float = 0.95,
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    GAE-based advantage and return estimation.
    Assumes a terminal value of 0 (episode ends cleanly).
    Returns: (returns, advantages), each shape (T,).
    """
    T = len(rewards)
    # Append V(s_{T+1}) = 0 for the post-terminal step
    values_ext = torch.cat([values, torch.zeros(1)])

    advantages = torch.zeros(T)
    gae = 0.0
    for t in reversed(range(T)):
        delta = rewards[t] + gamma * values_ext[t + 1] - values_ext[t]
        gae = delta + gamma * gae_lam * gae
        advantages[t] = gae

    returns = advantages + values
    return returns, advantages
Reward and Advantage Normalization

Reward model outputs vary significantly in scale depending on architecture and training. Normalizing rewards before computing returns stabilizes training by preventing the policy gradient step size from being dominated by the reward scale:

class RewardNormalizer:
    """
    Running mean/variance normalization of reward model scores.
    Applied before computing returns so that advantage magnitudes stay bounded.
    """
    def __init__(self, eps: float = 1e-8):
        self.mean = 0.0
        self.var  = 1.0
        self.n    = 0
        self.eps  = eps

    def update(self, scores: list[float]) -> None:
        """Welford online update for running statistics."""
        for x in scores:
            self.n += 1
            delta = x - self.mean
            self.mean += delta / self.n
            self.var = (self.var * (self.n - 1) + delta * (x - self.mean)) / self.n

    def normalize(self, score: float) -> float:
        return (score - self.mean) / (self.var ** 0.5 + self.eps)


def whiten_advantages(advantages: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Whiten (zero-mean, unit-variance) advantages over the batch.
    Critical for stable PPO updates: keeps gradient step sizes bounded
    regardless of absolute reward magnitude.
    """
    mean = advantages.mean()
    std  = advantages.std() + 1e-8
    return (advantages - mean) / std
Examples
Example 1: Manual Return Computation for a Sparse-Reward Episode
A 5-token response with reward model score 1.2 and per-step KL penalties of 0.05 each (KL coefficient

import torch

# 5-token response
policy_log_probs = torch.tensor([-2.1, -1.8, -2.3, -1.9, -2.0])
ref_log_probs    = torch.tensor([-2.0, -1.9, -2.1, -2.0, -2.1])
rm_score         = 1.2
kl_coef          = 0.05
values           = torch.tensor([0.9, 1.0, 1.0, 1.1, 1.1])

# Per-step KL: log(π_θ/π_ref) at each token
kl_per_token = policy_log_probs - ref_log_probs
# tensor([-0.1, +0.1, -0.2, +0.1, +0.1])

rewards = -kl_coef * kl_per_token
# Non-terminal: [-0.1, 0.1, -0.2, 0.1] * -0.05 = [+0.005, -0.005, +0.010, -0.005]
rewards[-1] += rm_score  # terminal: -0.05*(0.1) + 1.2 = 1.195

print('Per-token rewards:', rewards)
# tensor([ 0.005, -0.005,  0.010, -0.005,  1.195])

returns, advantages = compute_returns_and_advantages(rewards, values, gamma=1.0, gae_lam=0.95)
print('Returns:   ', returns.round(decimals=3))
print('Advantages:', advantages.round(decimals=3))
# Returns close to ~1.2 for all positions (dominated by terminal RM score)
# Advantages show where the critic's prediction differed from realized return
Example 2: Batch Return Computation Over Multiple Trajectories

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class RolloutBatch:
    trajectories: list[Trajectory] = field(default_factory=list)

    def compute_all_returns(
        self,
        kl_coef:  float = 0.05,
        gamma:    float = 1.0,
        gae_lam:  float = 0.95,
        normalize_rewards: bool = True,
    ) -> list[tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]]:
        """
        Process all trajectories: build rewards, compute GAE returns/advantages.
        Returns list of (returns, advantages) per trajectory.
        """
        # Optionally normalize RM scores across the batch
        rm_scores = [t.rm_score for t in self.trajectories]
        if normalize_rewards and len(rm_scores) > 1:
            mean = sum(rm_scores) / len(rm_scores)
            std  = (sum((s - mean)**2 for s in rm_scores) / len(rm_scores))**0.5 + 1e-8
            rm_scores = [(s - mean) / std for s in rm_scores]

        results = []
        for traj, norm_score in zip(self.trajectories, rm_scores):
            # Rebuild trajectory with normalized RM score
            normed_traj = Trajectory(
                prompt_ids=traj.prompt_ids,
                response_ids=traj.response_ids,
                policy_log_probs=traj.policy_log_probs,
                ref_log_probs=traj.ref_log_probs,
                values=traj.values,
                rm_score=norm_score,
            )
            rewards = compute_rewards(normed_traj, kl_coef=kl_coef)
            rets, advs = compute_returns_and_advantages(rewards, traj.values, gamma, gae_lam)
            results.append((rets, whiten_advantages(advs)))
        return results
Real-World Example: InstructGPT Reward Design
The InstructGPT paper (Ouyang et al., 2022) describes the reward formulation used to align GPT-3 with human preferences. The full reward at the terminal step is:

attrs.latex: R(x, y) = r_\phi(x, y) - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta^\mathrm{RL}(y \mid x)}{\pi^\mathrm{SFT}(y \mid x)}

content.text: Where
is the supervised fine-tuned reference model,
is the model being optimized, and
was selected by grid search. The reward model
was a 6B GPT model trained on pairwise human preference comparisons.

At scale, the InstructGPT training loop ran PPO on a 175B parameter model with a rollout buffer of 64 prompts per batch. Each prompt generated a response of up to 1024 tokens, yielding a trajectory of up to 1024 time steps with the reward distributed per-step as described above. The value function was a separate 6B model initialized from the SFT checkpoint, trained concurrently with the policy using returns computed by the GAE formula.
One practical finding from InstructGPT and subsequent work: with

set too low (e.g., 0.001), the policy rapidly exploits the reward model — generating syntactically valid but semantically repetitive or nonsensical completions that scored highly on the reward model but which human evaluators rated as worse than the SFT baseline. Increasing
to 0.02 restored alignment quality without significantly sacrificing reward model performance.
Common Mistakes
Using γ < 1 with Sparse Terminal Rewards
Setting
when the reward is only at the terminal step is a subtle but serious error. For a 200-token response, the return at step
is
. The reward signal is attenuated by 86% for the first generated token. With
— every token gets the full signal.
Omitting the KL Penalty

Without the KL term, the policy will rapidly diverge from the reference model in search of higher reward model scores. Within a few hundred steps, the policy typically collapses to mode-seeking behavior — generating the same few responses that the reward model scored highly during its training distribution, which are not necessarily high quality for novel prompts.
Not Normalizing Rewards or Advantages

If the reward model outputs scores in the range [0, 10] but the KL terms are O(0.01), the advantages will be dominated by the RM score and the gradient magnitude will be large. Conversely, if the RM is calibrated to [−1, 1] but a different checkpoint produced scores in [−5, 5], a training run that worked before will diverge after a model update. Always normalize RM scores within each batch and whiten advantages.
Using the Policy's Log-Probs to Compute KL for the Return and for the PPO Ratio
The per-step KL penalty in the reward must use the log-probs from the
rollout

(frozen at collection time). Using the live policy's log-probs, which change during the PPO update epochs, introduces inconsistency between the return target and the value function target across mini-batches.
Best Practices
Use γ = 1.0 for RLHF.
Text generation episodes are finite and all tokens are equally important for the response quality. Discounting is unnecessary and harmful with sparse terminal rewards.
Distribute KL over per-token rewards.
Adding
at each step rather than only at the terminal step provides denser gradient signal and speeds up value function learning.
Normalize RM scores within each rollout batch.
Use running statistics or batch statistics to keep the RM score distribution stable across training steps, decoupled from the absolute scale of the reward model's output.
Whiten advantages per mini-batch.
Subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation of advantages within each PPO mini-batch. This keeps the effective learning rate consistent across training.
Monitor the KL coefficient carefully.
Use an adaptive KL controller that increases
when the running KL divergence exceeds a target and decreases it when the KL is below target. This maintains stable optimization pressure without manual schedule tuning.
Set GAE λ between 0.9 and 0.98.
Values of
provide a good bias-variance tradeoff. Lower values reduce variance but may prevent early tokens from receiving adequate gradient signal when the critic is poorly calibrated.
Performance and Scalability
Computing returns and advantages is

per trajectory — the backward loop over time steps is fast compared to neural network forward passes. The dominant cost in the RLHF loop is not return computation but rollout generation (autoregressive inference for
tokens) and reward model inference.

Memory for rollout buffers scales with the number of trajectories times the maximum response length. At 64 trajectories of 1024 tokens each, storing log-probs, values, and rewards requires roughly 64 × 1024 × 4 × 4 bytes ≈ 1 GB for float32 — manageable, but at 512 trajectories and 4096 tokens, this grows to 32 GB and must be managed carefully with chunked storage or shorter response lengths.

Reward normalization state (running mean and variance) must be shared across all workers in a distributed training setup. A common pattern is to centralize the reward normalizer on the rank-0 process and broadcast updated statistics to all workers before each rollout batch.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Several libraries implement the reward and return pipeline out of the box:
TRL (Hugging Face).
The
PPOTrainer
handles reward computation, GAE, and advantage whitening. Reward shaping is done via a user-supplied
reward_model
callable.
OpenRLHF.
A scalable RLHF framework with distributed rollout support. Implements per-token KL rewards with configurable
and optional adaptive KL control.
DeepSpeed-Chat.
Microsoft's RLHF pipeline at scale. Includes hybrid engine support (inference + training on same GPUs) with integrated reward normalization across multi-node setups.
veRL.
A framework from ByteDance for RLHF at large scale (70B+ parameter models). Uses async rollout generation with separate actor and critic servers to maximize GPU utilization.
Using TRL to set up a custom reward function with KL penalty:

from trl import PPOTrainer, PPOConfig, AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
import torch

config = PPOConfig(
    model_name="gpt2",
    learning_rate=1.41e-5,
    kl_penalty="kl",          # use per-token KL in rewards
    init_kl_coef=0.05,        # β = 0.05
    adap_kl_ctrl=True,        # adaptive KL controller
    target_kl=6.0,            # target KL divergence per step
    gamma=1.0,                # no discounting
    lam=0.95,                 # GAE lambda
)

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(config.model_name)
model     = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained(config.model_name)
ref_model = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained(config.model_name)

trainer = PPOTrainer(config=config, model=model, ref_model=ref_model, tokenizer=tokenizer)

def reward_fn(response_tensors: list[torch.Tensor]) -> list[torch.Tensor]:
    """Custom reward: here using response length as a proxy."""
    return [torch.tensor(len(r) / 100.0) for r in response_tensors]

# In the training loop:
# query_tensors  = [tokenizer.encode(q, return_tensors='pt').squeeze() for q in queries]
# response_tensors = trainer.generate(query_tensors, max_new_tokens=128)
# rewards = reward_fn(response_tensors)  # list of scalar tensors
# stats = trainer.step(query_tensors, response_tensors, rewards)
# trainer.log_stats(stats, batch={}, rewards=rewards)
Comparison with Alternatives
Different alignment approaches handle rewards and returns very differently:
Method
Reward
Return
Key trade-off
PPO-RLHF
Explicit reward model score + KL
GAE-estimated returns; critic trained alongside
Maximum flexibility; high computational cost; reward hacking risk
DPO
Implicit reward via log-ratio of policy to reference
No explicit return; optimizes a pairwise loss directly
No rollouts needed; less flexible; cannot use online reward signal
REINFORCE
Explicit reward model (same as PPO)
Monte Carlo return; no critic needed
Simpler than PPO; very high variance; slower convergence
GRPO
Explicit reward (verifiable tasks or RM)
Group relative: baseline = mean return over K samples per prompt
No critic; low variance via group baseline; requires K rollouts per prompt
PRM-guided RL
Dense: process reward model scores each reasoning step
Full GAE with per-step rewards at step boundaries
Better credit assignment; requires expensive PRM training
Security and Reliability
The KL penalty in the return is the primary safety mechanism against reward hacking. Without it, or with too small a

, a sufficiently powerful policy will always find ways to game the reward model. Known failure modes include: generating repetitive text that exploits the reward model's statistical biases, producing responses at the reward model's preferred length distribution regardless of content, and producing syntactically valid but semantically empty completions that score well on form but not substance.

Additional reliability practices include monitoring the running KL divergence between the policy and reference throughout training (an alarm at KL > 20 nats often indicates reward hacking), tracking reward model score distributions to detect shift toward out-of-distribution high scores, and holding out a set of evaluation prompts whose reward model scores are not used for training to detect overfitting to the reward model.
At inference time, the return is not computed — only the policy is used. The return and value function are purely training constructs and add no runtime overhead to the deployed model.
Mental Model

Think of the reward as a grade on a test: it captures how well the completed work was judged, but it comes only at the end. The return is the GPA — a weighted accumulation of grades over time that the student is really trying to maximize. In classic RL with dense rewards, you get a grade after every homework problem, so the GPA builds steadily. In RLHF with sparse rewards, you only get graded on the final exam — which makes it harder to know which study habits (token choices) contributed to the grade.
The discount factor
controls how much future grades matter to the current decision. At
the student cares only about the immediate assignment; at
every future exam matters equally. For RLHF, where the only grade is the final exam (terminal reward), you must use
or the first "study sessions" (early tokens) barely receive any credit signal.

The per-step KL reward is like a mandatory attendance score for each class: it keeps the student showing up in a way consistent with good habits (staying close to the reference model), independent of what the final exam score turns out to be.
Key Takeaways
The
reward
is a scalar signal per step; the
return
is what the policy actually maximizes — the cumulative discounted sum of future rewards.
RLHF uses
sparse terminal rewards
: the reward model scores only the complete response; all intermediate tokens receive
(except for the per-step KL terms).
Set
for RLHF. Any
exponentially attenuates the terminal reward signal to early tokens, breaking training with sparse rewards.
The
KL penalty
in the return (
) is the primary guard against reward hacking and policy collapse.
Distributing the KL penalty as a
per-step reward
converts the purely sparse signal into a mildly dense one, improving value function learning and reducing advantage variance.
Returns are estimated using
GAE
with
, which interpolates between high-bias TD and high-variance Monte Carlo estimates.
Always normalize reward model scores within each batch and whiten advantages before the PPO update to ensure stable gradient magnitudes throughout training.

Alternatives to sparse rewards include process reward models (dense per-step scores) and group relative methods like GRPO, which avoid a separate value function by using the mean return across multiple samples of the same prompt as the baseline.
